author: pink_flowerz
content: is it ever possible that a a reorg changes the block.timestamp to a block.timestamp less than block n - 1, with block n being the reorged block. or does the invariant block.timestamp(n) < block.timestamp(n+1) always hold. i know it differs a bit accross the different non-evem blockchains

author: sky.cactus
content: `block.timestamp > block.parent.timstamp` is a hard requirement in every chain I know of.  Some have more constraints, like Ethereum *at the moment* is `> +12` (but this could change in the future).

author: sky.cactus
content: Note that this doesn't have anything to do with reorgs.  Just block validity logic.

author: potuz
content:
This can happen depending on what do you mean by block n and n-1. The important thing is that there may be many different valid **block n** and to make things more complicated, there is a difference between block n and slot n. In the current mainnet of Ethereum a situation that more or less resembles what you suggest is the following: 

There is a chain That looks like this with k a possibly large number. But these are **block numbers**
```
n-k <-.... <- n-2 <-- n-1 <-- n
```
If you look at slot numbers, these are very different with a bunch of them missing, for example it could look like 
```
(n-k, N-l) <- ... <- (n-2, N-5) <-- (n-1, N-1) <--- (n, N)
```
The second number is the slot number and the first is the block number. The requirement that Ethereum makes and that is alluded by <@301186049323958275> is that the timestamp of `n` has to be the exact timestamp of `N` that currently means `12 * N` seconds since genesis in December 1 2020. 

Suddenly you can see a different chain that becomes the head of the chain, and this chain is actually fuller than the one you had before as head: your reorg would look like:

```
(n-k, N-l) <- ... <- (n-2, N-5) <----------------------------------------- (n-1, N-1) <--- (n, N)
   \
    ------------------------(n-2. N-4) <---- (n-1, N-3) <---- (n, N-2) 
```
If this chain becomes head, the timestamp of the block at blockheight `n` will be below that of the timestamp of block height `n-1` in the previous head chain. 

Here I've played with the different between block and slot, but you can have nastier things if you start playing with k large enough so that there are two different blocks, of different heights **in the same slot** that are valid for the protocol. I'll leave that as a curious exercise, but probably Ben Edington already wrote an example in his book.
